title: Nitrogen doping induced metal-insulator transition with iso-symmetric character in rutile VO2
authors: Baichen Lin, Shanquan Chen, Yubo Zhang, Yangyang Si, Haoliang Huang, Chuanrui Huo, Frans Munnik, Yongqi Dong, Lu You, Jian Shao, Yu-Chieh Ku, Nguyen Nhat Quyen, Aryan Keshri, Zhenlin Luo, Weiwei Zhao, Chun-Fu Chang, Chih-Wei Luo, Sujit Das, Shiqing Deng, Chang-Yang Kuo, Zuhuang Chen
abstract: Metal-insulator transitions (MITs) in correlated oxides offer immense potential for next-generation Mottronic devices. However, their integration into practical applications is often hindered by the coupling of MITs with symmetry-lowering structural phase transitions, which limits switching speed and endurance. In this study, we engineered an iso-symmetric MIT on average in epitaxial rutile VO2 thin films via an in-situ nitrogen doping strategy. Nitrogen incorporation effectively suppresses V-V dimerization, enabling an iso-symmetric MIT, while preserving the original crystal symmetry. Furthermore, in-operando time-resolved optical reflectivity measurements revealed a shortened switching time in nitrogen-doped films, highlighting their enhanced performance. Our findings provide critical insights into the underlying mechanisms of MITs and introduce anion doping as a powerful tool for tailoring phase transitions in strongly correlated electron systems. This approach opens new avenues for the development of high-performance electronic and photonic devices.
summary: This paper reports the successful engineering of an iso-symmetric metal-insulator transition in VO2 thin films through nitrogen doping. By substituting nitrogen into oxygen sites, the researchers suppressed the structural dimerization that usually accompanies the transition, maintaining tetragonal symmetry throughout. This approach results in faster electronic switching speeds and provides evidence for a Mott-driven, rather than Peierls-driven, transition. These findings are significant for developing high-performance, high-speed electronic and photonic 'Mottronic' devices.
main_problem: The metal-insulator transition (MIT) in VO2 is typically coupled to a symmetry-lowering structural phase transition, which limits the switching speed and endurance of Mottronic devices. The study aims to achieve an iso-symmetric MIT where the electronic transition occurs without changing the crystal symmetry.
main_result: Nitrogen doping in VO2 thin films suppresses V-V dimerization, enabling an iso-symmetric, tetragonal-to-tetragonal MIT driven by a Mott mechanism. This transition exhibits faster switching kinetics compared to undoped VO2.
method: The researchers used reactive pulsed laser deposition (PLD) for nitrogen doping, combined with structural characterization (XRD, STEM, Raman), chemical analysis (XPS, ERDA), and ultrafast time-resolved optical reflectivity. Theoretical validation was performed using DFT (r2SCAN) and DFT+DMFT.
model_or_system: Epitaxial VO2 thin films grown on (001) TiO2 substrates, where nitrogen ions (N3-) substitutionally replace oxygen (O2-) sites to induce p-type carrier density.
key_observables: Transition temperature (T_MIT), lattice parameters, carrier density, V-V dimerization, transient reflectivity (delta R/R), and orbital occupation (d_parallel and pi* bands).
important_parameters: Nitrogen doping concentration (~1%), transition temperature (~280 K), switching time (~207 ps for N-doped vs ~264 ps for pure VO2), and bandgap (~0.89 eV).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the nitrogen is incorporated substitutionally at oxygen sites without significant interstitial presence or oxygen vacancies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the fabrication strategy, N-doping schematic, and evidence of film quality via XRD and STEM. Figure 2 presents electrical resistivity, RSM, and Raman spectroscopy showing symmetry preservation. Figure 3 compares ultrafast transient reflectivity spectra and decay times.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the fabrication strategy and structural verification of N-doped VO2, moves to characterizing the electrical and structural properties of the phase transition, presents ultrafast dynamics of the switching process, and concludes with theoretical modeling (DFT/DMFT) of the orbital-driven Mott mechanism.
why_interesting: The paper demonstrates a way to decouple electronic and structural phase transitions using chemical doping, providing a playground for studying pure Mott physics and orbital-driven transitions in a controlled, high-speed manner.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Fabrication of high-quality N-doped VO2 epitaxial thin films by reactive pulsed-laser deposited technique in N2-containing growth atmosphere. (A) Strategies for constructing an iso- symmetric phase transition in VO2 films. Left panel illustrates the conventional phase transition from metallic rutile to insulating monoclinic VO2. Middle panel shows the metallic monoclinic phase of VO2 induced by photon excitation or interfacial effects. Right panel demonstrates the rutile insulating phase induced by nitrogen doping. The yellow lines represent the existent (solid) and absent (dashed) V-V dimers, respectively. (B) Schematic illustration of nitrogen doping in rutile VO2, where...

Figure 2. Iso-symmetric electronic phase transition in 25 nm-thick N-doped VO2 thin films. (A) Temperature-dependent electrical resistivity of N-doped VO2 thin films with thicknesses of 10 nm, 25 nm, 45 nm, and 70 nm. The grey band highlights the consistent MIT temperature (TMIT) for films up to 45 nm. The TMIT of bulk single-crystal VO2 (~340 K) is reproduced from ref. 38 with permission. Copyright 2007, Elsevier. (B) Room-temperature RSM of the N-doped VO2 thin film around the (002), (112), and (202) TiO2 reflections. The (002), (112), and (202) reflection peaks of the N-doped VO2 thin film show the same Qz value, which is highlighted via a red dashed line. (C) Temperature-dependent XRD...

Figure 3. Enhanced MIT speed in 25 nm-thick N-doped VO2 thin films due to iso-symmetric phase transition. (A and B) Comparison of the temperature-dependent Raman spectroscopy and resistivity for (A) 25 nm-thick pure VO2 and (B) the 25 nm-thick N-doped VO2 thin films, highlighting the coupling behavior between SPT and MIT. (C to E) Ultrafast dynamics of pure VO2 and N-doped VO2 thin films. (C) Peak amplitude of the transient reflectivity change (R/R) spectra at 0 ps (see Figure S12) of pure VO2 and N- doped VO2 thin films with cooling and warming processes. The -R/R of the N-doped VO2 and pure VO2 thin films are plotted into the left and right Y-axis, respectively, which is indicated by...

Figure 4. Orbital-dominated MIT in rutile VO2. (A) Schematics of orbital occupation behavior in rutile VO2. Top: Diagram of t2g orbitals, with 𝒅𝒙𝟐−𝒚𝟐 orbitals forming 𝒅|| band and 𝒅𝒙𝒛/𝒅𝒚𝒛 orbitals contributing to 𝝅∗ band. Site 1 is rotated 90˚ around the c-axis to align with Site 2. Bottom: Local coordinate system defining the d-orbitals on V atoms. Isosurfaces denote the electron redistribution during the metallic-to-insulating transition, expressed as ∆𝒏= 𝒏𝒊𝒏𝒔𝒖𝒍𝒂𝒕𝒐𝒓−𝒏𝒎𝒆𝒕𝒂𝒍, where red and blue indicate electron depletion and accumulation, respectively.
